lesson.title: Permutations & Combinations
lesson.body: **Permutations** count arrangements where order matters: P(n, r) = n! / (n−r)!. Example: how many ways to arrange 3 of 5 books = 5×4×3 = 60. **Combinations** count selections where order does NOT matter: C(n, r) = n! / (r!(n−r)!). Example: choosing 3 from 5 people for a committee = 10. Key question: does the order of selection matter? If yes → permutation; if no → combination.

rooms.question: A race has 8 runners. How many ways can the top 3 finishes (gold, silver, bronze) be assigned?
rooms.explanation: Order matters (different medals = different outcomes). P(8,3) = 8×7×6 = 336. The 56 answer is C(8,3) = 56 (ignoring order). The 40,320 is 8! (all 8 in order, not just top 3).

rooms.question: A committee of 3 is chosen from 7 people. How many committees are possible?
rooms.explanation: Order doesn't matter (a committee is a set, not a ranked list). C(7,3) = 7×6×5/(3×2×1) = 210/6 = 35. The 210 is the numerator only (forgetting to divide by 3!). The 840 is P(7,3) = 7×6×5×4 — wrong formula.

rooms.question: Should you use permutation or combination? 'How many ways can 4 students sit in 4 chairs in a row?'
answers: Combination — the same people regardless of seat
Permutation — the seat positions are distinct
Either — the answer is the same
Neither — this uses the counting principle
rooms.explanation: Sitting ABCD differs from BACD (Alice in seat 1 vs seat 2 is a different arrangement). Order matters → permutation. P(4,4) = 4! = 24. If you just asked 'how many groups of 4 can be formed from 4 people?' — that would be 1 (combination, C(4,4)=1).

rooms.question: A pizza shop has 10 toppings. How many ways can you choose exactly 3 toppings?
rooms.explanation: C(10,3) = (10×9×8)/(3×2×1) = 720/6 = 120. Toppings are a set — pepperoni+mushroom+onion is the same pizza regardless of which was chosen first. The 720 is the numerator only (= P(10,3)).

rooms.question: Sort these into Permutation (order matters) vs Combination (order doesn't matter):
items: Choosing 5 cards from a deck for a hand → Combination
First, second, third place in a contest → Permutation
Selecting 3 flavors for a sampler plate → Combination
rooms.explanation: Permutations (order matters): contest rankings (1st ≠ 2nd) and PIN codes (1234 ≠ 4321). Combinations (order doesn't matter): card hand (same 5 cards regardless of dealing order) and flavor sampler (same 3 flavors regardless of pick order).

boss.name: The Permutation Wraith
boss.taunt: n! ways to fail. You will find them all.

questions.question: How many 4-letter arrangements (no repeats) can be made from the letters A, B, C, D, E?
questions.explanation: P(5,4) = 5×4×3×2 = 120. Order matters (ABCD ≠ DCBA). The 24 = 4! error only considers 4 letters (missing the 5th option for each slot). The 5 answer is C(5,1) — choosing only 1 at a time.

questions.question: From a group of 6 boys and 4 girls, a committee of 3 boys and 2 girls is chosen. How many committees are possible?
questions.explanation: C(6,3) = 6!/(3!3!) = 20 ways to choose the boys. C(4,2) = 4!/(2!2!) = 6 ways to choose the girls. Total = 20 × 6 = 120. Multiply because the boy-choice and girl-choice are independent. The 48 error uses P(6,3)/P(4,2) incorrectly.

questions.question: How many different 5-card hands from a 52-card deck contain exactly 2 aces?
